# Sub-team Test Strategy: Persistence Layer

## iDaVIE Workspace Save/Restore Infrastructure

### 1\. Introduction

This document describes the test strategy used by our sub-team. It covers the architectural constraints that influenced our testing, the three-tier testing model used to verify functionality at increasing levels of integration into the system, proposed coverage targets, the dependency isolation (pure-C\# persistence layer with no Unity dependency) approach that makes pure unit testing possible without a running Unity Editor, and the CI quality gates that enforce these standards automatically on every commit.

### 2\. Testing Strategy

#### 2.1 Architectural Layers as a Test Constraint

The persistence system is split into four layers, each only allowed to talk to the layer below it never above. The innermost layers (Domain and Application) have no Unity code in them at all. This means they can be tested with standard C\# test tools on any machine, without needing Unity installed or a VR headset plugged in. Only the outermost layer (Adapters) is allowed to touch Unity, so the core save/load logic stays hardware-independent and testable in a CI pipeline.

The Domain, Application, and Infrastructure layers are implemented as a standalone .NET 8 class library (refactoring-examples/Persistence/), with its test suite in Tests/PersistenceTests/. This library has no Unity dependencies, its only external reference is Newtonsoft.Json for serialization, and can be compiled, tested, and statically analysed by standard .NET tooling(dotnet build and test) without a Unity installation. The Unity-specific adapter classes (Assets/Scripts/Persistence/Adapters/) are excluded from this automated test suite, this is tested through Tier 2 (Play mode) and Tier 3 (built executable) testing described below.

####  2.2 Dependency Inversion at the Adapter Boundary

  Each Unity adapter implements a pure-C\# interface defined in the Application layer:

  IDataIoStateAdapter → DataIoStateAdapter (Unity)  
  IRenderingStateAdapter → RenderingStateAdapter (Unity)  
  IFeatureStateAdapter → FeatureStateAdapter (Unity)  
  IInteractionStateAdapter → InteractionStateAdapter (Unity)  
  IGuiStateAdapter → GuiStateAdapter (Unity)

* SaveWorkspaceUseCase doesn't know anything about Unity or the scene. It just asks "give me the current state" through IWorkspaceStateCollector (an interface). It doesn't care what's behind that interface.  
* Test doubles implementing these interfaces can be implemented directly in unit tests, producing fully deterministic saves without touching the scene graph.  
* Instead of passing the real  UnityWorkspaceStateCollector (which needs a live Unity scene), we pass one of these substitutes that implements the same IWorkspaceStateCollector interface but returns controlled, predictable hardcoded data.

   

#### 2.3 Deferred Dependency Resolution

* A key testing decision was to pass the collector as a function Func\<IWorkspaceStateCollector\>) rather than a fixed instance. This means SaveWorkspaceUseCase does not hold a direct reference to any Unity object. Instead, it calls the function at  the start of each save cycle to get a fresh collector/snapshot returning null if no FITS file is currently loaded, which causes the save to be silently skipped.  
* In production, the factory checks VolumeInputController.ActiveDataSet at save time, correctly handling the case where a dataset is loaded or unloaded during a session. In unit tests, the function is replaced with a simple lambda that returns a stub collector or null, covering both the normal save path and the skip path without needing a running Unity scene.

####  2.4 Threading at the Adapter Boundary

The autosave timer is driven by Unity's InvokeRepeating call inside PersistenceController, which guarantees execution on the Unity main thread. This is a deliberate choice: Unity APIs including system property reads on MonoBehaviour subclasses, are only safe to call from the main thread(we discovered this at testing), and violations throw UnityException. A background thread timer (such as System.Threading.Timer) would silently swallow these exceptions, creating a failure mode where unit tests pass and no errors are logged in Play mode, yet no snapshot is ever written.

Because InvokeRepeating is a Unity API, the timer itself is not unit-testable. Threading correctness is instead verified by the Play mode tier of testing: if the main-thread guarantee were broken, no snapshot files would appear in the ring buffer directory. This shows a wider architectural principle: thread-boundary errors require integration testing to surface and are invisible to pure unit tests that execute synchronously on a single thread because of this we ended out taking a three tier approach to testing with 3 types of testing traditional Unit testing, Integration testing and actual User Interaction.

### 3\. Three-Tier Testing Model

We used three tiers of testing that gave us coverage across different levels of the software stack.

#### Tier 1 Automated Unit Tests (No Unity Dependency)

**Scope:** Domain, Application, and Infrastructure layers only. All tests run against the \`refactoring-examples/Persistence/\` .NET 8 library.

**Execution:** \`dotnet test tests/PersistenceTests\` runs in under five seconds, no GUI, no scene loading, executable in any CI environment.

![][image1]

**Test suite composition (78 tests):**

| Test class | Number of Unit Tests | Focus |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| WorkspaceValidatorTests | 35 | All validation rules: field clamping, enum defaults, critical-failure detection |
| SnapshotRingTests | 9 | FIFO push/pop/eviction at capacity; atomic rename; corrupt slot skipping; \`ring.json\` update |
| SnapshotSerializerTests | 13 | Round-trip fidelity; checksum computation; checksum mismatch rejection; truncated file rejection |
| WorkspaceStubFactoryTests | 14 | Each \`WorkspaceProfile\` produces the correct non-null/null sub-sections |
| MigrationCoordinatorTests | 6 | v1.0→v1.1 migration preserves all fields; unknown major version rejected | |

**Deterministic round-trip tests:** \`SnapshotSerializerTests\` constructs a \`WorkspaceSnapshot\` with known field values, serialises it to a JSON string, deserialises it back, and asserts field-level equality. Because the snapshot uses plain-C\# DTOs with no Unity types, every assertion is deterministic and produces no false negatives from floating-point rounding (values are written and read back at full \`float\`/\`double\` precision via Newtonsoft.Json).

**Property-based approach for migration tests:** \`MigrationCoordinatorTests\` generates snapshots with version tags set to"1.0.0"\` and asserts that after migration all fields from the v1.0 contract survive unchanged in the v1.1 output, new additions are initialised to zero values. This is a structural property  "migration is a field-preserving transformation" rather than a specific-value assertion, which makes the tests robust to future additions to the DTO structure such as new attributes.

**Fakes and test doubles:** When a test needs a fake object it just builds a real WorkspaceSnapshot with hardcoded values directly in the test. You can see this in MakeFullSnapshot() in SnapshotSerializerTests.cs. No framework needed because the domain objects are plain C\# classes with no Unity dependencies.The \`SnapshotRing\` writes to a \`System.IO.Path.GetTempPath()\` directory created per-test and deleted in \`TearDown\`, keeping tests hermetic and leaving no artefacts on the CI runner. Hermetic means each test gets its own fresh folder with a unique GUID in the name, so tests can't interfere with each other and nothing is left behind on the machine after the test run finishes.

#### Tier 2 Unity Editor Play Mode

**Scope:** Full stack including Unity adapters, but without VR hardware or a compiled executable. We simulated interactions and use cases and viewed behaviours through console log

**Execution:** Developer-run in Unity Editor 2021.3.45f2 on a development machine. SteamVR is present but no headset is connected; SteamVR initialises against a null driver, producing expected OpenVR errors that do not affect persistence behaviour.

**Verified behaviours:**

1\. **Startup logging.** On entering Play mode, the Console must show:  
   \- \`\[Persistence\] Snapshot directory: \<path\>\`  
![][image2]  
   
 \`\[Persistence\] Autosave started (interval \= 20s).\`  
![][image3]

   \- Either \`\[Persistence\] Clean start.\` or \`\[Persistence\] Crash detected attempting recovery from last snapshot.\`  
![][image4]

2\. \*\*Autosave write.\*\* After loading a \`.fits\` file and waiting the configured interval (20 s default), the Console must show \`\[Persistence\] Autosave snapshot written.\` and a new \`snapshot\_XX.json\` file must appear in \`%APPDATA%\\iDaVIE\\workspaces\\default\\snapshots\\\`.  
![][image5]  
![][image6]

3\. \*\*Ring buffer eviction.\*\* After 10 autosave cycles, \`snapshot\_00.json\` through \`snapshot\_09.json\` must all exist and \`ring.json\` must record \`"count": 10, "head": 0\` (head has wrapped).

4\. \*\*Crash detection.\*\* With Unity stopped via the OS process terminator (not the Editor Stop button, which invokes \`OnApplicationQuit\` and performs a clean shutdown), the \`session.lock\` file must persist. On the next Play session, the startup log must read \`\[Persistence\] Crash detected.\`

5\. \*\*Manual crash-lock test.\*\* As an alternative to OS-level process termination, a \`session.lock\` file can be placed in the snapshot directory before pressing Play. The same \`Crash detected\` message must appear, confirming that the \`CrashDetector\` reads the lock file independently of how the previous session ended.

echo crash \> "$env:APPDATA\\iDaVIE\\workspaces\\default\\snapshots\\session.lock"

#### Tier 3 Built Executable with SteamVR Null Driver

**Scope:** Compiled Windows standalone build, SteamVR launched with null driver (\`steamvr.vrsettings\`: \`"activateMultipleDrivers": true\`), full session lifecycle.

**Verified behaviours:**

1\. All 10 ring buffer slots fill correctly over a \~200-second session.  
2\. Force-killing the process with Task Manager (using End Process, not End Task, to prevent WM\_CLOSE graceful shutdown) leaves \`session.lock\` intact.  
3\. Relaunch of the executable surfaces the crash recovery path.  
4\. Manually corrupting one snapshot file (truncating to zero bytes) causes the ring to skip that slot and load the next-newest valid snapshot, with a warning in the Console.  
5\. Removing the source \`.fits\` file from disk and relaunching produces disconnected mode: the UI is preserved, a relink prompt is shown, and no \`NullReferenceException\` occurs in the persistence layer.

### 4\. Coverage Targets

Coverage is measured over the \`refactoring-examples/Persistence/\` library only (Tier 1 tests). Unity adapter code is excluded because it cannot be exercised without a Unity runtime.

| Metric | Target | Reasoning |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Line coverage | ≥ 85 % | Achievable on pure-C\# library, the remaining 15% headroom accounts for things like defensive null checks that can never realistically be triggered |
| Branch coverage | ≥ 75 % | Validator and ring buffer logic have high branch density(if else forks); target is less than line coverage and reflects realistic coverage of error-path branches |
| Condition coverage (Modified Condition / Decision Coverage) | ≥ 70 % on \`WorkspaceValidator\` | Validator contains the densest boolean logic AND and OR logic within conditional statements; MC/DC ensures each condition independently influences the outcome  |

### 5\. Mocking and Dependency Isolation

 5.1 No External Mocking Framework

No mocking library is used. Each adapter interface has only two methods (Capture() and Restore()), so fakes are simple inline implementations returning hardcoded DTOs. 

5.2 File System Isolation

SnapshotRing and SnapshotSerializer write real files. Each test creates a unique temporary directory using Path.Combine(Path.GetTempPath(), Guid.NewGuid().ToString()) in \[SetUp\] and deletes it in \[TearDown\], preventing collisions between parallel test runs and leaving no files behind on the CI runner.

5.3 Clock Isolation

Serializer tests avoid timestamp variability by using a hardcoded fixed date rather than DateTime.UtcNow. Ring buffer tests only assert ordering between timestamps, not exact values. No clock abstraction is needed.

5.4 Adapter Boundary Contract

The adapter interfaces are the boundary between unit-testable and Unity-dependent code. Their correctness is verified indirectly: the WorkspaceValidator test suite covers the full range of valid and invalid DTO values, so any adapter that produces an out-of-range field will be caught at the unit test level without requiring Unity.

### 6\. CI Quality Gates

  6\. CI Quality Gates

  The following gates are enforced in .github/workflows/ci.yaml and block merge to main on failure.

| Gate | Job | Failure Condition |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| .NET 8 build  | build-dotnet  | dotnet build refactoring-examples exits non-zero  |
| 78 unit tests pass | persistence-tests | Any test fails   |
| No compiler warnings | build-dotnet | warnaserror flag causes build failure on any warning |


* The persistence-tests job depends on build-dotnet and runs on ubuntu-latest, confirming that the persistence library has no Windows-specific file path assumptions (SnapshotRing and SnapshotSerializer use Path.Combine throughout) making it **portable**.  
* Coverage measurement is not yet automated in CI. The targets stated in Section 4 serve as manual review thresholds rather than enforced gates.  
* Unity-layer testing (Tier 2 and Tier 3\) is not automated in CI and remains a manual gate executed by the persistence sub-team before each sprint review. The checklist for manual sign-off is the set of behaviours listed in Sections 3.2 and 3.3 above.

### 7\. Summary

Our sub-team's test strategy rests on the architectural decision to keep all domain, application, and infrastructure logic free of Unity dependencies. This single constraint enables 78 automated unit tests to run in a standard CI pipeline, providing rapid feedback on the correctness of validation rules, ring buffer mechanics, serialisation fidelity, and schema migration logic. Play mode and executable testing then verify the Unity integration and threading model that cannot be exercised in isolation. Together, the three tiers provide confidence across the full depth of the persistence stack, from individual field-level validation through to crash recovery in a live VR session.
